title: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness
authors: Rebecca M. Baron, Anthony F. Massaro

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness | ASSESSMENT OF ILLNESS SEVERITY
items.title: Utilization and Limitations of SOI Scoring Systems in ICU
items.content: SOI scoring systems like SOFA, APACHE, and qSOFA are used for assessing illness severity in critically ill patients. While validated for population studies and administrative purposes, their predictive accuracy for individual patient outcomes is unclear.
keyPoints: Numerous SOI scoring systems have been developed over the past three decades.
Their utility may be more applicable toward defining patient populations for clinical trial outcomes and broader epidemiologic studies.
SOI scores are useful in guiding hospital administrative policies, directing resource allocation, and assessing ICU care quality over time.
Scoring system validations assume that age, chronic medical illnesses, and physiological derangements increase mortality rates.
All existing SOI scoring systems are derived from patients already admitted to the ICU.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness | ASSESSMENT OF ILLNESS SEVERITY

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness | ASSESSMENT OF ILLNESS SEVERITY
items.title: Role of qSOFA in Prognostication of Sepsis Outcomes
items.content: The quick SOFA (qSOFA) scoring system is increasingly used for prognosticating sepsis outcomes, reflecting revised consensus guidelines.
keyPoints: There has been more recent interest in the use of a 'quick' SOFA, or qSOFA, scoring system for prognostication of sepsis outcomes.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness | ASSESSMENT OF ILLNESS SEVERITY

items.subtopic: THE SOFA SCORING SYSTEM | THE APACHE II SCORING SYSTEM
items.title: SOFA Score: Indicator of Sepsis Severity and Mortality Risk
items.content: The Sequential Organ Failure Assessment (SOFA) score evaluates six organ systems, with scores ranging from 0 to 4 based on dysfunction severity. It is repeatable daily, and an increase in the SOFA score correlates with higher mortality. An increase of at least two points from baseline in the setting of suspected or documented infection indicates sepsis.
keyPoints: SOFA score ranges from 0 to 4 per organ system
Rising scores correlate with increasing mortality
An increase of at least two points from baseline is diagnostic of sepsis in the presence of infection
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · THE SOFA SCORING SYSTEM | THE APACHE II SCORING SYSTEM

items.subtopic: THE SOFA SCORING SYSTEM | THE APACHE II SCORING SYSTEM
items.title: APACHE II: Predicting Mortality in ICU Patients
items.content: The Acute Physiology and Chronic Health Evaluation (APACHE) II system is widely used to predict hospital mortality. It incorporates age, type of ICU admission, chronic health issues, and 12 physiological variables measured during the first 24 hours post-ICU admission.
keyPoints: APACHE II uses age, ICU admission type, chronic health problems, and 12 physiologic variables
Scores are derived from worst values in the first 24 hours after ICU admission
Predicted mortality is calculated using APACHE II score, emergency surgery need, and disease-specific categories
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · THE SOFA SCORING SYSTEM | THE APACHE II SCORING SYSTEM

items.subtopic: OTHER SCORING SYSTEMS | INITIAL EVALUATION (part 1)
items.title: Utilization of Scoring Systems in Critical Illness
items.content: Electronic health record scoring systems are being increasingly studied for their potential to incorporate large, real-time patient data sets and alert providers about patients at risk for sepsis or poor outcomes.
keyPoints: Scoring systems can integrate larger and real-time data sets from electronic health records.
These systems may help in identifying patients at risk for sepsis and/or poor clinical outcomes.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · OTHER SCORING SYSTEMS | INITIAL EVALUATION (part 1)

items.subtopic: OTHER SCORING SYSTEMS | INITIAL EVALUATION (part 1)
items.title: Assessment of Shock in Hypotensive Patients
items.content: Shock is characterized by multisystem end-organ hypoperfusion, with clinical indicators including reduced MAP, tachycardia, and cool extremities. Initial evaluation should assess cardiac output adequacy.
keyPoints: Shock involves multisystem end-organ hypoperfusion.
Clinical indicators: reduced mean arterial pressure (MAP), tachycardia, tachypnea, cool skin and extremities, altered mental status, oliguria.
Assessment of cardiac output is crucial; signs include narrow pulse pressure and cool extremities ('cold shock').
Increased cardiac output indicates decreased systemic vascular resistance.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · OTHER SCORING SYSTEMS | INITIAL EVALUATION (part 1)

items.subtopic: OTHER SCORING SYSTEMS | INITIAL EVALUATION (part 1)
items.title: Management of Hypotensive Patients with Increased Cardiac Output
items.content: In patients with increased cardiac output, the focus is on identifying causes of decreased systemic vascular resistance. Initial management includes volume resuscitation and vasopressors.
keyPoints: High-cardiac-output hypotension often requires targeted volume resuscitation to achieve euovolemia.
Vasopressors may be necessary to maintain vascular tone.
Common causes include sepsis, liver failure, severe pancreatitis, adrenal insufficiency, burns, trauma, anaphylaxis, thyrotoxicosis, and peripheral arteriovenous shunts.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · OTHER SCORING SYSTEMS | INITIAL EVALUATION (part 1)

items.subtopic: INITIAL EVALUATION (part 2) | MECHANICAL VENTILATORY SUPPORT (SEE ALSO CHAP. 313)
items.title: Importance of Early Resuscitation in Shock
items.content: Early aggressive resuscitation based on initial assessment is crucial for improving survival rates, especially in septic and cardiogenic shock. The use of ultrasound can aid in assessing fluid responsiveness and cardiac performance.
keyPoints: Aggressive targeted resuscitation should be instituted quickly after initial assessment to improve survival in septic and cardiogenic shock.
Ultrasound may predict a fluid-responsive state through inferior vena cava collapse in spontaneously breathing patients.
Targeted, not excessive, fluid resuscitation is essential to avoid over-resuscitation.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · INITIAL EVALUATION (part 2) | MECHANICAL VENTILATORY SUPPORT (SEE ALSO CHAP. 313)

items.subtopic: INITIAL EVALUATION (part 2) | MECHANICAL VENTILATORY SUPPORT (SEE ALSO CHAP. 313)
items.title: Criteria for Mechanical Ventilation in Shock
items.content: Mechanical ventilation may be necessary during shock to manage acute hypoxemic respiratory failure and ventilatory failure, which can result from increased respiratory load or decreased lung compliance.
keyPoints: Indications for mechanical ventilation include acute hypoxemic respiratory failure and ventilatory failure often seen with cardiogenic shock, septic shock with pneumonia, or ARDS.
Increased respiratory muscle workload due to inefficient activity in shock may necessitate early intubation and mechanical support.

Signs of respiratory distress warranting mechanical ventilation include inability to speak full sentences, accessory muscle use, paradoxical abdominal movement, tachypnea (>40 breaths/min), and decreasing respiratory rate despite increased drive.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · INITIAL EVALUATION (part 2) | MECHANICAL VENTILATORY SUPPORT (SEE ALSO CHAP. 313)

items.subtopic: INITIAL EVALUATION (part 2) | MECHANICAL VENTILATORY SUPPORT (SEE ALSO CHAP. 313)
items.title: Managing Hypotension During Mechanical Ventilation
items.content: Mechanical ventilation can lead to hypotension due to factors like impeded venous return and reduced catecholamine secretion, necessitating careful management with IV fluids or vasopressors.
keyPoints: Hypotension may occur during mechanical ventilation due to positive-pressure ventilation effects on venous return and endogenous catecholamine levels.
Patients with right heart dysfunction or pulmonary hypertension are at increased risk of diminished cardiac output from increased right ventricular afterload.
IV volume administration should be considered for fluid-responsive patients, and vasopressor support may be necessary peri-intubation.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · INITIAL EVALUATION (part 2) | MECHANICAL VENTILATORY SUPPORT (SEE ALSO CHAP. 313)

items.subtopic: RESPIRATORY FAILURE | TYPE I: ACUTE HYPOXEMIC RESPIRATORY FAILURE
items.title: Low Tidal Volume Ventilation Reduces Mortality in ARDS
items.content: In patients with Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS), using low tidal volume ventilation significantly reduces mortality compared to traditional high tidal volume strategies. This approach minimizes ventilator-induced volutrauma by preventing alveolar overdistention.
keyPoints: Traditional ventilator strategy uses a large tidal volume of 12 mL/kg of ideal body weight.
Low tidal volume strategy uses 6 mL/kg of ideal body weight.
Study showed reduced mortality in low-tidal-volume group (31%) compared to high-tidal-volume group (39.8%).
Large tidal volumes may cause ARDS in patients without initial problems.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · RESPIRATORY FAILURE | TYPE I: ACUTE HYPOXEMIC RESPIRATORY FAILURE

items.subtopic: RESPIRATORY FAILURE | TYPE I: ACUTE HYPOXEMIC RESPIRATORY FAILURE
items.title: Prone Positioning and Fluid-Conservative Strategies Improve Outcomes in Severe ARDS
items.content: For patients with severe ARDS, prone positioning has been shown to improve survival rates. Additionally, a fluid-conservative management strategy, maintaining low central venous pressure (CVP) or pulmonary capillary wedge pressure (PCWP), results in fewer days on mechanical ventilation compared to a fluid-liberal approach.
keyPoints: Prone positioning improves survival in severe ARDS and is used in COVID-19 cases.
Fluid-conservative strategy involves low CVP or PCWP, leading to fewer ventilation days.
Fluid-liberal strategy maintains higher CVP or PCWP.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · RESPIRATORY FAILURE | TYPE I: ACUTE HYPOXEMIC RESPIRATORY FAILURE

items.subtopic: TYPE II RESPIRATORY FAILURE: HYPERCAPNEIC RESPIRATORY FAILURE
items.title: Mechanisms and Management of Hypercapnic Respiratory Failure
items.content: Hypercapnic respiratory failure arises from alveolar hypoventilation, leading to ineffective CO2 elimination. It can be due to impaired CNS drive ('won't breathe'), neuromuscular dysfunction ('can't breathe'), or increased respiratory loads. Causes include drug overdose, brainstem injury, myasthenia gravis, and bronchospasm. Management focuses on addressing the underlying cause and may involve noninvasive positive-pressure ventilation in specific cases.
keyPoints: Causes: impaired CNS drive (e.g., drug overdose), neuromuscular dysfunction (e.g., myasthenia gravis), increased respiratory loads (e.g., bronchospasm).
Management: reverse underlying cause, consider noninvasive positive-pressure ventilation for certain patients.
Noninvasive ventilation is beneficial in chronic obstructive pulmonary disease exacerbations.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · TYPE II RESPIRATORY FAILURE: HYPERCAPNEIC RESPIRATORY FAILURE | TYPE III RESPIRATORY FAILURE: LUNG ATELECTASIS

items.subtopic: TYPE III RESPIRATORY FAILURE: LUNG ATELECTASIS
items.title: Management of Lung Atelectasis and Perioperative Respiratory Failure
items.content: Type III respiratory failure, or lung atelectasis, often occurs perioperatively due to decreased functional residual capacity post-anesthesia. Management includes frequent position changes, chest physiotherapy, upright positioning, pain control, and potentially noninvasive positive-pressure ventilation to reverse regional atelectasis.
keyPoints: Occurs commonly in the perioperative period due to anesthesia-induced decreases in functional residual capacity.
Management: positional changes, chest physiotherapy, upright positioning, pain management.
Noninvasive positive-pressure ventilation can be used to treat regional atelectasis.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · TYPE II RESPIRATORY FAILURE: HYPERCAPNEIC RESPIRATORY FAILURE | TYPE III RESPIRATORY FAILURE: LUNG ATELECTASIS

items.subtopic: TYPE IV RESPIRATORY FAILURE: METABOLIC DEMANDS | CARE OF THE MECHANICALLY VENTILATED PATIENT
items.title: Understanding Type IV Respiratory Failure and Its Management
items.content: Type IV respiratory failure arises from the hypoperfusion of respiratory muscles, often seen in shock states. This condition can lead to significant metabolic demands as up to 40% of cardiac output may be redirected to respiratory muscles. Mechanical ventilation helps redistribute cardiac output away from these muscles, allowing for treatment of underlying shock and acidosis.
keyPoints: Type IV respiratory failure is due to hypoperfusion of respiratory muscles in shock.
Respiratory muscles can consume up to 40% of cardiac output in this setting.
Mechanical ventilation redistributes cardiac output away from respiratory muscles.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · TYPE IV RESPIRATORY FAILURE: METABOLIC DEMANDS | CARE OF THE MECHANICALLY VENTILATED PATIENT

items.subtopic: TYPE IV RESPIRATORY FAILURE: METABOLIC DEMANDS | CARE OF THE MECHANICALLY VENTILATED PATIENT
items.title: Optimal Sedation and Analgesia in Mechanically Ventilated Patients
items.content: Mechanically ventilated patients often require sedatives and analgesics, with opiates being the mainstay for pain control. Nonbenzodiazepine sedatives are preferred due to their lower association with delirium compared to benzodiazepines. Neuromuscular blocking agents like cisatracurium may be used as a last resort in cases of severe ARDS or ventilator dyssynchrony.
keyPoints: Opiates are the mainstay for analgesia in mechanically ventilated patients.
Nonbenzodiazepine sedatives are preferred to reduce delirium risk.
Neuromuscular blocking agents should be a last resort due to potential prolonged weakness.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · TYPE IV RESPIRATORY FAILURE: METABOLIC DEMANDS | CARE OF THE MECHANICALLY VENTILATED PATIENT

items.subtopic: TYPE IV RESPIRATORY FAILURE: METABOLIC DEMANDS | CARE OF THE MECHANICALLY VENTILATED PATIENT
items.title: Criteria for Spontaneous Breathing Trials in Mechanically Ventilated Patients
items.content: Daily spontaneous breathing trials (SBTs) are crucial to assess readiness for extubation. Criteria include stable oxygenation with a Pao2/FIO2 ratio >200, PEEP ≤5 cm H2O, intact cough and airway reflexes, and absence of vasopressors or sedatives. SBTs involve breathing through the endotracheal tube without significant support for 30–120 minutes.
keyPoints: SBTs are conducted if Pao2/FIO2 >200 and PEEP ≤5 cm H2O.
Criteria include intact cough and airway reflexes, no vasopressors or sedatives.
SBT failure is indicated by respiratory rate >35/min for >5 min, O2 saturation <90%, heart rate changes, blood pressure extremes, or increased anxiety.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · TYPE IV RESPIRATORY FAILURE: METABOLIC DEMANDS | CARE OF THE MECHANICALLY VENTILATED PATIENT

items.subtopic: MULTIORGAN SYSTEM FAILURE | MONITORING IN THE ICU
items.title: Understanding Multiorgan System Failure in the ICU
items.content: Multiorgan system failure (MOSF) is characterized by the dysfunction or failure of two or more organs simultaneously, often occurring in critical conditions such as severe sepsis and shock. The ability to manage single-organ failures has shifted mortality from early stages to later in ICU stays.
keyPoints: MOSF involves simultaneous dysfunction/failure of at least two organs.
Commonly associated with severe sepsis, shock, pancreatitis, and trauma.
Organ failure must persist beyond 24 hours for MOSF diagnosis.
Mortality risk increases with the number of failing organs.
Prognosis worsens with increased duration of organ failure.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · MULTIORGAN SYSTEM FAILURE | MONITORING IN THE ICU

items.subtopic: MULTIORGAN SYSTEM FAILURE | MONITORING IN THE ICU
items.title: Key Monitoring Techniques in ICU for Respiratory and Circulatory Function
items.content: In the ICU, monitoring respiratory and circulatory systems is crucial due to their frequent failure in critically ill patients. Arterial blood-gas analysis remains the gold standard for assessing respiratory function by measuring pH, $P_{aO_2}$, $P_{CO_2}$, and O$_2$ saturation.
keyPoints: Respiratory gas exchange evaluation is routine in critical illness.
Arterial blood-gas analysis measures pH, $P_{aO_2}$, $P_{CO_2}$, and O$_2$ saturation directly.
Assessment of lung functions: oxygenation and CO$_2$ elimination.
Venous pH can approximate arterial pH but is less accurate.
Noninvasive monitoring methods are often used due to limitations of arterial sampling.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · MULTIORGAN SYSTEM FAILURE | MONITORING IN THE ICU

items.subtopic: PULSE OXIMETRY | RESPIRATORY SYSTEM MECHANICS | CIRCULATORY STATUS
items.title: Understanding Pulse Oximetry Limitations in Diverse Populations
items.content: Pulse oximetry, a noninvasive method for monitoring oxygen saturation, can overestimate readings in patients with darker skin. This discrepancy necessitates correlation with arterial PaO₂ to ensure accurate assessment of oxygenation status.
keyPoints: Pulse oximetry uses light absorption differences at 660 nm and 940 nm wavelengths.
Overestimation of oxygen saturation occurs in patients with darker skin, especially noted during the COVID pandemic.
Correlation with arterial PaO₂ is crucial for accurate assessment.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · PULSE OXIMETRY | RESPIRATORY SYSTEM MECHANICS | CIRCULATORY STATUS

items.subtopic: PULSE OXIMETRY | RESPIRATORY SYSTEM MECHANICS | CIRCULATORY STATUS
items.title: Assessing Airway Resistance and Compliance in Mechanical Ventilation
items.content: During volume-controlled mechanical ventilation, the gradient between peak and plateau airway pressures helps quantify airway resistance. A significant difference (>10–15 cmH₂O) indicates increased airway resistance at a constant inspiratory flow rate of 1 L/s.
keyPoints: Peak airway pressure is influenced by both airway resistance and respiratory system compliance.
Plateau pressure reflects only respiratory system compliance, unaffected by airway resistance.
A gradient >10–15 cmH₂O between peak and plateau pressures indicates increased airway resistance.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · PULSE OXIMETRY | RESPIRATORY SYSTEM MECHANICS | CIRCULATORY STATUS

items.subtopic: PULSE OXIMETRY | RESPIRATORY SYSTEM MECHANICS | CIRCULATORY STATUS
items.title: Evaluating Respiratory System Compliance in Pathologic Conditions
items.content: Respiratory system compliance, normally around 100 mL/cmH₂O, can be reduced by conditions affecting either the lungs or chest wall. Elevated peak and plateau pressures with a normal gradient suggest decreased compliance due to pathologies like pneumonia or pleural effusions.
keyPoints: Normal respiratory system compliance is ~100 mL/cmH₂O.
Conditions such as pleural effusion, pneumothorax, and increased abdominal girth reduce chest wall compliance.
Lung compliance may be reduced by pneumonia, pulmonary edema, or interstitial lung disease.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · PULSE OXIMETRY | RESPIRATORY SYSTEM MECHANICS | CIRCULATORY STATUS

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness | ASSESSMENT OF ILLNESS SEVERITY
items.question: Which statement best describes the utility of severity-of-illness (SOI) scoring systems in predicting individual patient outcomes?
options: They are highly accurate for predicting individual patient outcomes at the bedside.
Their utility is more applicable toward defining patient populations for clinical trial outcomes and broader epidemiologic studies.
They are primarily used to determine the exact time of discharge from the ICU.
They replace the need for ongoing clinical bedside assessment.
items.explanation: SOI scoring systems have limited utility in predicting individual patient outcomes at the bedside, but they help define populations for studies and administrative purposes.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness | ASSESSMENT OF ILLNESS SEVERITY

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness | ASSESSMENT OF ILLNESS SEVERITY
items.question: What is a common use of SOI scores in hospital settings?
options: To predict individual patient recovery times.
To guide hospital administrative policies and resource allocation.
To determine the specific interventions needed for each patient.
To replace clinical assessments with automated scoring.
items.explanation: SOI scores are used to guide hospital administrative policies, direct resources, and assess ICU care quality over time.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness | ASSESSMENT OF ILLNESS SEVERITY

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness | ASSESSMENT OF ILLNESS SEVERITY
items.question: Which scoring systems are most commonly utilized in assessing severity of illness?
items.explanation: The most commonly utilized scoring systems are the SOFA (Sequential Organ Failure Assessment) and the APACHE (Acute Physiology and Chronic Health Evaluation).
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness | ASSESSMENT OF ILLNESS SEVERITY

items.subtopic: THE SOFA SCORING SYSTEM | THE APACHE II SCORING SYSTEM
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a criterion used in the quick SOFA (qSOFA) score to screen for poor outcomes from sepsis?
items.explanation: qSOFA uses respiratory rate, altered mental status, and systolic blood pressure as criteria; heart rate is not included.

items.subtopic: THE SOFA SCORING SYSTEM | THE APACHE II SCORING SYSTEM
items.question: What does an increase of at least two points in the SOFA score from baseline indicate according to recent sepsis guidelines?
options: Diagnostic of sepsis in suspected or documented infection
items.explanation: An increase of at least two points in the SOFA score from baseline is diagnostic of sepsis.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · THE SOFA SCORING SYSTEM | THE APACHE II SCORING SYSTEM

items.subtopic: THE SOFA SCORING SYSTEM | THE APACHE II SCORING SYSTEM
items.question: Which scoring system uses age, type of ICU admission, chronic health problems, and 12 physiologic variables to derive a score?
items.explanation: The APACHE II system uses these factors to derive its score.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · THE SOFA SCORING SYSTEM | THE APACHE II SCORING SYSTEM

items.subtopic: OTHER SCORING SYSTEMS | INITIAL EVALUATION (part 1)
items.question: Which clinical indicators suggest the presence of shock due to multisystem end-organ hypoperfusion?
options: Reduced mean arterial pressure, tachycardia, and warm extremities
Increased jugular venous pressure, oliguria, and cool skin
Reduced mean arterial pressure, tachycardia, and cool skin
Widened pulse pressure, rapid capillary refill, and warm extremities
items.explanation: Shock is indicated by reduced MAP, tachycardia, tachypnea, cool skin, altered mental status, and oliguria.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · OTHER SCORING SYSTEMS | INITIAL EVALUATION (part 1)

items.subtopic: OTHER SCORING SYSTEMS | INITIAL EVALUATION (part 1)
items.question: What does a narrow pulse pressure in a hypotensive patient indicate?
items.explanation: A narrow pulse pressure correlates with reduced stroke volume, indicating diminished cardiac output.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · OTHER SCORING SYSTEMS | INITIAL EVALUATION (part 1)

items.subtopic: OTHER SCORING SYSTEMS | INITIAL EVALUATION (part 1)
items.question: Which bedside metric is a better predictor of fluid responsiveness in hypotensive patients?
options: Static measures of right atrial pressure
Change in jugular venous pressure with respiration
items.explanation: The change in right atrial pressure as a function of spontaneous respiration is a better predictor of fluid responsiveness.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · OTHER SCORING SYSTEMS | INITIAL EVALUATION (part 1)

items.subtopic: OTHER SCORING SYSTEMS | INITIAL EVALUATION (part 1)
items.question: Which condition is the most common cause of high-cardiac-output hypotension?
items.explanation: The most common cause of high-cardiac-output hypotension is sepsis.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · OTHER SCORING SYSTEMS | INITIAL EVALUATION (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a common category of shock mentioned in the passage?
items.explanation: Neurogenic shock is not listed as one of the most common categories of shock in the passage.

items.question: What is a potential consequence of positive-pressure ventilation during mechanical ventilatory support?
items.explanation: Positive-pressure ventilation can impede venous return, contributing to hypotension.

items.question: What is one goal of targeted fluid resuscitation during initial evaluation?
options: To prevent or minimize end-organ injury
items.explanation: The goal is to reestablish adequate tissue perfusion and thus to prevent or minimize end-organ injury.

items.subtopic: RESPIRATORY FAILURE | TYPE I: ACUTE HYPOXEMIC RESPIRATORY FAILURE
items.question: Which condition is NOT typically associated with Type I acute hypoxemic respiratory failure?
items.explanation: Type I respiratory failure occurs due to alveolar flooding and ventilation-perfusion mismatch, not directly from heart failure.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · RESPIRATORY FAILURE | TYPE I: ACUTE HYPOXEMIC RESPIRATORY FAILURE

items.subtopic: RESPIRATORY FAILURE | TYPE I: ACUTE HYPOXEMIC RESPIRATORY FAILURE
items.question: What is the primary concern with using traditional ventilator strategies in ARDS patients?
items.explanation: Traditional ventilator strategies with large tidal volumes can cause alveolar overdistention, termed ventilator-induced volutrauma.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · RESPIRATORY FAILURE | TYPE I: ACUTE HYPOXEMIC RESPIRATORY FAILURE

items.subtopic: RESPIRATORY FAILURE | TYPE I: ACUTE HYPOXEMIC RESPIRATORY FAILURE
items.question: What has been shown to improve survival in patients with severe ARDS?
items.explanation: Prone positioning has been shown to improve survival in patients with severe ARDS.

items.question: Which of the following is a cause of diminished CNS drive to breathe in hypercapnic respiratory failure?
items.explanation: Drug overdose is a cause of diminished CNS drive to breathe.

items.question: What is a common treatment for lung atelectasis in the perioperative period?
items.explanation: Frequent changes in position are a common treatment for lung atelectasis.

items.explanation: Central nervous system drive is not a subclassification of increased load on the respiratory system.

items.subtopic: TYPE IV RESPIRATORY FAILURE: METABOLIC DEMANDS | CARE OF THE MECHANICALLY VENTILATED PATIENT
items.question: Which of the following is a primary reason for respiratory distress in patients with Type IV respiratory failure?
options: Hypoperfusion of respiratory muscles due to shock
Excessive oxygen consumption by respiratory muscles
Inadequate sedation leading to increased autonomic activity
items.explanation: Type IV respiratory failure often results from hypoperfusion of respiratory muscles in patients experiencing shock.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · TYPE IV RESPIRATORY FAILURE: METABOLIC DEMANDS | CARE OF THE MECHANICALLY VENTILATED PATIENT

items.subtopic: TYPE IV RESPIRATORY FAILURE: METABOLIC DEMANDS | CARE OF THE MECHANICALLY VENTILATED PATIENT
items.question: What is the mainstay therapy for analgesia in mechanically ventilated patients?
items.explanation: Opiates are the mainstay of therapy for analgesia in mechanically ventilated patients.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · TYPE IV RESPIRATORY FAILURE: METABOLIC DEMANDS | CARE OF THE MECHANICALLY VENTILATED PATIENT

items.subtopic: TYPE IV RESPIRATORY FAILURE: METABOLIC DEMANDS | CARE OF THE MECHANICALLY VENTILATED PATIENT
items.question: Which sedative is NOT preferred due to its association with increased delirium and worse outcomes in mechanically ventilated patients?
items.explanation: Benzodiazepines are associated with increased delirium and worse patient outcomes, hence nonbenzodiazepine sedatives are preferred.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · TYPE IV RESPIRATORY FAILURE: METABOLIC DEMANDS | CARE OF THE MECHANICALLY VENTILATED PATIENT

items.subtopic: TYPE IV RESPIRATORY FAILURE: METABOLIC DEMANDS | CARE OF THE MECHANICALLY VENTILATED PATIENT
items.question: Under what condition should a patient undergo a spontaneous breathing trial (SBT)?
options: Cough and airway reflexes are impaired
Pao2/Fio2 >200, PEEP ≤5 cm H2O, and no sedatives or vasopressors
items.explanation: A patient should undergo an SBT if oxygenation is stable (Pao2/Fio2 >200), PEEP ≤5 cm H2O, cough and airway reflexes are intact, and no sedatives or vasopressors are being administered.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · TYPE IV RESPIRATORY FAILURE: METABOLIC DEMANDS | CARE OF THE MECHANICALLY VENTILATED PATIENT

items.subtopic: MULTIORGAN SYSTEM FAILURE | MONITORING IN THE ICU
items.question: Which condition is NOT typically associated with multiorgan system failure in the ICU?
items.explanation: Multiorgan system failure is commonly associated with severe conditions like sepsis, shock, and trauma, not mild dehydration.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · MULTIORGAN SYSTEM FAILURE | MONITORING IN THE ICU

items.subtopic: MULTIORGAN SYSTEM FAILURE | MONITORING IN THE ICU
items.question: What must persist beyond 24 hours to be considered organ failure in the context of multiorgan system failure?
items.explanation: Organ failure must persist beyond 24 hours to be considered as part of multiorgan system failure.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · MULTIORGAN SYSTEM FAILURE | MONITORING IN THE ICU

items.subtopic: MULTIORGAN SYSTEM FAILURE | MONITORING IN THE ICU
items.question: What is the 'gold standard' for evaluating respiratory gas exchange in critically ill patients?
items.explanation: Arterial blood-gas analysis is the 'gold standard' for evaluating respiratory gas exchange.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · MULTIORGAN SYSTEM FAILURE | MONITORING IN THE ICU

items.subtopic: PULSE OXIMETRY | RESPIRATORY SYSTEM MECHANICS | CIRCULATORY STATUS
items.question: Which wavelength of light does oxyhemoglobin reflect more effectively in pulse oximetry?
items.explanation: Oxyhemoglobin reflects light more effectively at 660 nm.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · PULSE OXIMETRY | RESPIRATORY SYSTEM MECHANICS | CIRCULATORY STATUS

items.subtopic: PULSE OXIMETRY | RESPIRATORY SYSTEM MECHANICS | CIRCULATORY STATUS
items.question: What does the difference between peak and plateau airway pressures during volume-controlled ventilation indicate?
items.explanation: The difference indicates airway resistance.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · PULSE OXIMETRY | RESPIRATORY SYSTEM MECHANICS | CIRCULATORY STATUS

items.subtopic: PULSE OXIMETRY | RESPIRATORY SYSTEM MECHANICS | CIRCULATORY STATUS
items.question: What is the normal respiratory system compliance value?
items.explanation: Normal respiratory system compliance is ~100 mL/cmH₂O.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · PULSE OXIMETRY | RESPIRATORY SYSTEM MECHANICS | CIRCULATORY STATUS

items.subtopic: PULSE OXIMETRY | RESPIRATORY SYSTEM MECHANICS | CIRCULATORY STATUS
items.question: What phenomenon results from insufficient time for alveolar emptying before the next inspiratory cycle?
items.explanation: Insufficient time for alveolar emptying results in auto-PEEP.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · PULSE OXIMETRY | RESPIRATORY SYSTEM MECHANICS | CIRCULATORY STATUS

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness | ASSESSMENT OF ILLNESS SEVERITY
items.statement: SOI scoring systems are validated tools for predicting individual patient outcomes at the bedside.
items.explanation: SOI scoring systems are not clearly predictive of individual patient outcomes; their utility is more in population studies and administrative guidance.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness | ASSESSMENT OF ILLNESS SEVERITY

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness | ASSESSMENT OF ILLNESS SEVERITY
items.statement: The COVID-19 pandemic has increased the need for effective critical care practices.
items.explanation: The COVID-19 pandemic heightened the priority for effective critical care and post-ICU support.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness | ASSESSMENT OF ILLNESS SEVERITY

items.subtopic: THE SOFA SCORING SYSTEM | THE APACHE II SCORING SYSTEM
items.statement: qSOFA is recommended as a sepsis diagnostic screening tool.
items.explanation: qSOFA is not currently recommended as a sepsis diagnostic tool.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · THE SOFA SCORING SYSTEM | THE APACHE II SCORING SYSTEM

items.subtopic: THE SOFA SCORING SYSTEM | THE APACHE II SCORING SYSTEM
items.statement: The APACHE II score includes the worst values of 12 physiologic variables in the first 24 hours after ICU admission.
items.explanation: APACHE II uses the worst values for each of the 12 physiologic variables within the first 24 hours.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · THE SOFA SCORING SYSTEM | THE APACHE II SCORING SYSTEM

items.subtopic: OTHER SCORING SYSTEMS | INITIAL EVALUATION (part 1)
items.statement: In patients with increased cardiac output, the reduced blood pressure is due to decreased systemic vascular resistance.
items.explanation: Increased cardiac output with hypotension suggests decreased SVR as the cause of low blood pressure.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · OTHER SCORING SYSTEMS | INITIAL EVALUATION (part 1)

items.subtopic: OTHER SCORING SYSTEMS | INITIAL EVALUATION (part 1)
items.statement: Static measures of right atrial pressure are reliable predictors of fluid responsiveness.
items.explanation: Dynamic changes in right atrial pressure with respiration, not static measures, predict fluid responsiveness.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · OTHER SCORING SYSTEMS | INITIAL EVALUATION (part 1)

items.subtopic: INITIAL EVALUATION (part 2) | MECHANICAL VENTILATORY SUPPORT (SEE ALSO CHAP. 313)
items.statement: Excess fluid resuscitation is beneficial in the treatment of shock.
items.explanation: The passage states that excess fluid resuscitation is likely not beneficial.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · INITIAL EVALUATION (part 2) | MECHANICAL VENTILATORY SUPPORT (SEE ALSO CHAP. 313)

items.subtopic: INITIAL EVALUATION (part 2) | MECHANICAL VENTILATORY SUPPORT (SEE ALSO CHAP. 313)
items.statement: Mechanical ventilation may increase the percentage of cardiac output dedicated to respiratory muscles.
items.explanation: In shock with respiratory distress, the percentage of cardiac output for respiratory muscles can increase significantly.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · INITIAL EVALUATION (part 2) | MECHANICAL VENTILATORY SUPPORT (SEE ALSO CHAP. 313)

items.subtopic: RESPIRATORY FAILURE | TYPE I: ACUTE HYPOXEMIC RESPIRATORY FAILURE
items.statement: The mortality rate for ARDS has remained consistently high at 50-70%.
items.explanation: Changes in patient care have reduced the mortality rates closer to 30%.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · RESPIRATORY FAILURE | TYPE I: ACUTE HYPOXEMIC RESPIRATORY FAILURE

items.subtopic: RESPIRATORY FAILURE | TYPE I: ACUTE HYPOXEMIC RESPIRATORY FAILURE
items.statement: A new global definition for ARDS includes reliance on chest radiography.
items.explanation: The new global definition for ARDS does not rely upon chest radiography.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · RESPIRATORY FAILURE | TYPE I: ACUTE HYPOXEMIC RESPIRATORY FAILURE

items.subtopic: TYPE II RESPIRATORY FAILURE: HYPERCAPNEIC RESPIRATORY FAILURE | TYPE III RESPIRATORY FAILURE: LUNG ATELECTASIS
items.statement: Noninvasive positive-pressure ventilation is contraindicated in patients with hemodynamic instability.
items.explanation: Hemodynamic instability is a contraindication for noninvasive positive-pressure ventilation.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · TYPE II RESPIRATORY FAILURE: HYPERCAPNEIC RESPIRATORY FAILURE | TYPE III RESPIRATORY FAILURE: LUNG ATELECTASIS

items.subtopic: TYPE II RESPIRATORY FAILURE: HYPERCAPNEIC RESPIRATORY FAILURE | TYPE III RESPIRATORY FAILURE: LUNG ATELECTASIS
items.statement: Lung atelectasis is a cause of hypercapnic respiratory failure.
items.explanation: Lung atelectasis causes Type III respiratory failure, not hypercapnic (Type II) respiratory failure.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · TYPE II RESPIRATORY FAILURE: HYPERCAPNEIC RESPIRATORY FAILURE | TYPE III RESPIRATORY FAILURE: LUNG ATELECTASIS

items.subtopic: TYPE IV RESPIRATORY FAILURE: METABOLIC DEMANDS | CARE OF THE MECHANICALLY VENTILATED PATIENT
items.statement: Neuromuscular blocking agents should be used as a first-line treatment for ventilator dyssynchrony.
items.explanation: Neuromuscular blocking agents are typically used as a last resort when aggressive sedation fails to achieve patient–ventilator synchrony.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · TYPE IV RESPIRATORY FAILURE: METABOLIC DEMANDS | CARE OF THE MECHANICALLY VENTILATED PATIENT

items.subtopic: TYPE IV RESPIRATORY FAILURE: METABOLIC DEMANDS | CARE OF THE MECHANICALLY VENTILATED PATIENT
items.statement: Noninvasive ventilation as a rescue strategy after failed extubation is always associated with better outcomes than immediate reintubation.
items.explanation: The use of noninvasive ventilation as a rescue strategy may be associated in some patients with worse outcomes than those obtained with immediate reintubation.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · TYPE IV RESPIRATORY FAILURE: METABOLIC DEMANDS | CARE OF THE MECHANICALLY VENTILATED PATIENT

items.subtopic: MULTIORGAN SYSTEM FAILURE | MONITORING IN THE ICU
items.statement: Mortality risk decreases with the accrual of failing organs in multiorgan system failure.
items.explanation: Mortality risk increases with the accrual of failing organs.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · MULTIORGAN SYSTEM FAILURE | MONITORING IN THE ICU

items.subtopic: MULTIORGAN SYSTEM FAILURE | MONITORING IN THE ICU
items.statement: Venous pH obtained through an indwelling central venous line can approximate arterial pH.
items.explanation: Venous pH can be used to approximate arterial pH.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · MULTIORGAN SYSTEM FAILURE | MONITORING IN THE ICU

items.subtopic: PULSE OXIMETRY | RESPIRATORY SYSTEM MECHANICS | CIRCULATORY STATUS
items.statement: Pulse oximetry accurately measures oxygen saturation regardless of skin color.
items.explanation: Pulse oximetry overestimates oxygen saturation in patients with darker skin.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · PULSE OXIMETRY | RESPIRATORY SYSTEM MECHANICS | CIRCULATORY STATUS

items.subtopic: PULSE OXIMETRY | RESPIRATORY SYSTEM MECHANICS | CIRCULATORY STATUS
items.statement: Auto-PEEP can result from obstruction of distal airways.
items.explanation: Auto-PEEP occurs due to insufficient time for alveolar emptying, often caused by distal airway obstruction.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · PULSE OXIMETRY | RESPIRATORY SYSTEM MECHANICS | CIRCULATORY STATUS

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness | ASSESSMENT OF ILLNESS SEVERITY
items.assertion: SOI scoring systems are derived from patients who have already been admitted to the ICU.
items.reason: Scoring system validations assume that age, chronic illnesses, and physiological derangements increase mortality rates.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why SOI scoring systems are derived from ICU patients.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness | ASSESSMENT OF ILLNESS SEVERITY

items.subtopic: OTHER SCORING SYSTEMS | INITIAL EVALUATION (part 1)
items.assertion: A hypotensive patient with clinical signs of increased cardiac output requires vasopressors.
items.reason: The reduced blood pressure in these patients is due to decreased systemic vascular resistance.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the need for vasopressors.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · OTHER SCORING SYSTEMS | INITIAL EVALUATION (part 1)

items.subtopic: OTHER SCORING SYSTEMS | INITIAL EVALUATION (part 1)
items.assertion: A hypotensive patient with signs of reduced cardiac output should be assessed for intravascular volume status.
items.reason: Decreased jugular venous pressure is a reliable predictor of fluid responsiveness.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false because dynamic changes in JVP with respiration are better predictors.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · OTHER SCORING SYSTEMS | INITIAL EVALUATION (part 1)

items.subtopic: INITIAL EVALUATION (part 2) | MECHANICAL VENTILATORY SUPPORT (SEE ALSO CHAP. 313)
items.assertion: Early intubation and mechanical ventilation are often required in patients with shock.
items.reason: They help relieve the work of breathing and allow redistribution of cardiac output to vital organs.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · INITIAL EVALUATION (part 2) | MECHANICAL VENTILATORY SUPPORT (SEE ALSO CHAP. 313)

items.subtopic: INITIAL EVALUATION (part 2) | MECHANICAL VENTILATORY SUPPORT (SEE ALSO CHAP. 313)
items.assertion: Patients with shock should always receive aggressive fluid resuscitation.
items.reason: Targeted fluid resuscitation is the goal to prevent over-resuscitation.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because aggressive fluid resuscitation without targeting can lead to harm.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · INITIAL EVALUATION (part 2) | MECHANICAL VENTILATORY SUPPORT (SEE ALSO CHAP. 313)

items.subtopic: RESPIRATORY FAILURE | TYPE I: ACUTE HYPOXEMIC RESPIRATORY FAILURE
items.assertion: Mechanical ventilation of patients with ARDS may propagate lung injury.
items.reason: Repeated stretching and overdistention of injured alveoli during mechanical ventilation can further injure the lung.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why mechanical ventilation can propagate lung injury.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · RESPIRATORY FAILURE | TYPE I: ACUTE HYPOXEMIC RESPIRATORY FAILURE

items.subtopic: TYPE IV RESPIRATORY FAILURE: METABOLIC DEMANDS | CARE OF THE MECHANICALLY VENTILATED PATIENT
items.assertion: Mechanically ventilated patients frequently require sedatives and analgesics.
items.reason: Opiates are the mainstay of therapy for analgesia in mechanically ventilated patients.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why sedatives and analgesics are frequently required.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · TYPE IV RESPIRATORY FAILURE: METABOLIC DEMANDS | CARE OF THE MECHANICALLY VENTILATED PATIENT

items.subtopic: TYPE IV RESPIRATORY FAILURE: METABOLIC DEMANDS | CARE OF THE MECHANICALLY VENTILATED PATIENT
items.assertion: Neuromuscular blocking agents should be used routinely for all mechanically ventilated patients.
items.reason: They result in pharmacologic paralysis without altering mental status, necessitating sedative-induced amnesia.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because neuromuscular blocking agents are typically a last resort; the reason is true but does not justify routine use.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · TYPE IV RESPIRATORY FAILURE: METABOLIC DEMANDS | CARE OF THE MECHANICALLY VENTILATED PATIENT

items.subtopic: MULTIORGAN SYSTEM FAILURE | MONITORING IN THE ICU
items.assertion: Critically ill patients often die later in the ICU stay after initial stabilization.
items.reason: The ability to support single-organ failure has reduced early mortality rates.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason explains why patients often die later in the ICU.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · MULTIORGAN SYSTEM FAILURE | MONITORING IN THE ICU

items.subtopic: PULSE OXIMETRY | RESPIRATORY SYSTEM MECHANICS | CIRCULATORY STATUS
items.assertion: Auto-PEEP leads to diminished lung compliance.
items.reason: Alveolar overdistention from auto-PEEP results in increased plateau airway pressures.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · PULSE OXIMETRY | RESPIRATORY SYSTEM MECHANICS | CIRCULATORY STATUS

items.subtopic: PULSE OXIMETRY | RESPIRATORY SYSTEM MECHANICS | CIRCULATORY STATUS
items.assertion: Increased airway resistance is indicated by a normal gradient between peak and plateau pressures.
items.reason: The gradient between these pressures reflects airway resistance during volume-controlled ventilation.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; increased airway resistance results in an elevated gradient, not a normal one.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Critical Illness · PULSE OXIMETRY | RESPIRATORY SYSTEM MECHANICS | CIRCULATORY STATUS
